content.text: Preference Centers for AI and GenAI Settings

A consent dialog captures a user's initial choices; a preference center lets them revisit, adjust, and extend those choices at any time. For GenAI features, the preference center must also expose functional settings — retrieval scope, model selection, response tone — alongside privacy choices, so that all AI-related configuration lives in one auditable place rather than scattered across application settings.
Preference vs Consent: What Goes Where
Yes — must be recorded with timestamp + policy version
Preferred model, response length, language, tone
Which knowledge bases the RAG retriever searches
Partial — restricts data use; requires consent for expansion
Yes — must be documented as data processing policy
Do not store consent signals as mutable preferences.
Consent records must be immutable and append-only. Preference stores are mutable and can be overwritten — mixing them destroys the audit trail.

Enterprise deployments need multiple scopes. An organisation-wide admin sets the outer bounds; departments and teams narrow them; individual users choose within what remains. Lower scopes can restrict further but cannot exceed what the higher scope permits.
Sets hard locks (e.g., FINE_TUNE_TRAINING permanently off for all users). Also sets global defaults that cascade down.
Dept admin can restrict the retrieval scope to department knowledge bases, disable personalisation for compliance-sensitive teams, etc. Cannot loosen org locks.
Team lead can set defaults for new members joining the team. No policy authority — purely defaults, which users may override.
Individual can adjust any preference that has not been locked by a higher scope. Changes take effect immediately.
Whether user has consented to RAG retrieval
Whether user has consented to training data inclusion
Whether interaction telemetry may be collected
Whether a persistent user profile may be maintained
LLM model ID or "auto" to let router decide
BCP 47 language tag for response language
Knowledge base IDs the retriever may search; [] = all permitted
Cap on number of retrieved chunks per query

The preference center API exposes a read endpoint (returns the resolved preferences for a user, with each setting annotated with its origin scope and whether it is locked) and write endpoints for each scope level.

When a user updates a preference — especially retrieval scope or consent — downstream systems must apply the new value immediately. The preference center publishes a domain event; consumers subscribe and update their cached state.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import Any

class ScopeAwareRetriever:
    """Retrieves only from knowledge bases the user's resolved preferences permit."""

    def __init__(self, base_retriever, pref_center: PreferenceCenterService) -> None:
        self._retriever = base_retriever
        self._prefs = pref_center

    def retrieve(
        self,
        query: str,
        *,
        user_id: str,
        team_id: str,
        dept_id: str,
        org_id: str,
        top_k: int = 5,
    ) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
        center = self._prefs.get_for_user(user_id, team_id, dept_id, org_id)
        allowed_kbs: list[str] = next(
            (p.value for p in center.preferences if p.key == "rag_scope"),
            [],   # empty list = all permitted KBs
        )
        return self._retriever.retrieve(
            query,
            top_k=top_k,
            filter_kb_ids=allowed_kbs if allowed_kbs else None,
        )

Organisation administrators can set preferences as locked, preventing any lower scope from overriding them. This is how compliance teams enforce non-negotiable data governance rules across all users without needing per-user configuration.
All users treated as non-consented regardless of individual consent
All requests routed to on-premise model; external model calls blocked
Retriever never queries non-EU knowledge bases
All responses include footnote citations; user cannot disable
Locked preferences must be visible to users.
The preference center UI must show locked settings as read-only and explain why they are locked (e.g., "Set by your organisation's data policy"). Hidden locks damage trust.
Single panel for all AI settings.
Privacy consents, functional preferences, and retrieval scope must all appear in one place, not scattered across separate settings pages.
Display whether each setting comes from org policy, department defaults, team defaults, or the user's own choice.
Locked settings show a padlock icon and a brief explanation, and their inputs are disabled.
When a user saves a preference change, show what will change ("From now on, the AI will only search the Engineering knowledge base").

The panel must clearly distinguish between consent toggles (with their legal implications) and functional preferences (purely UX). Using identical toggle controls for both confuses users about the legal significance of consent signals.

A preference center for AI settings combines two distinct concerns: consent signals (legally significant, immutable records) and functional preferences (mutable UX choices). Scope inheritance — from organisation to department to team to user — ensures that compliance policies are enforced without requiring per-user administration, while still giving individual users control within the bounds that policy allows. Preference changes propagate as domain events so retrieval scope, model routing, and analytics gates update immediately without requiring a re-login.
